title: Chicken and Mushroom Pie
subtitle: Tender chicken and earthy mushrooms in a creamy sauce, topped with golden puff pastry
description: A deeply comforting British classic — pieces of chicken and chestnut mushrooms bathed in a velvety white wine and cream sauce, sealed beneath a burnished puff-pastry lid. The filling is made first on the hob, cooled slightly, then topped and baked until the pastry puffs and crisps. The whole thing comes together with a standard set of storecupboard ingredients and rewards patience with a proper pub-style pie.
sourceNotes: British pie tradition, common in pub menus and home cooking since the mid-twentieth century. No single canonical source; the format follows the standard covered-pie method used by British cookery writers including Delia Smith and Jane Grigson.

recipeTools.notes: For dicing the chicken and slicing the mushrooms.
Wide enough to brown the chicken in a single layer — crowding causes steaming rather than browning.
A 24–26 cm oval or round pie dish, roughly 5 cm deep. Ceramic or enamel work well.
For the egg wash that gives the lid its colour.

glossaryTerms.definition: The browned, sticky residue left in a pan after sautéing meat. Deglazing with liquid lifts the fond and carries all that flavour into the sauce.
Beaten egg brushed over raw pastry before baking to promote an even, deep-golden colour.

steps: Heat the oven to 200 °C fan (220 °C / gas 7). Season the chicken pieces with salt and pepper.
Set a large frying pan over a high heat with a splash of oil. Brown the chicken in two batches, 3–4 minutes per batch, turning once. Transfer to a plate — it will finish cooking in the oven.
Reduce the heat to medium. Add the butter to the same pan. Once foaming, cook the onion for 5 minutes until soft. Add the garlic and thyme leaves and cook for 1 minute more.
Add the mushrooms and cook for 4–5 minutes, stirring occasionally, until golden and any liquid has evaporated.
Scatter the flour over the vegetables and stir for 1 minute to cook out the raw taste.

Pour in the white wine and stir to lift the fond from the base of the pan. Let it bubble for 1 minute, then add the hot stock, a ladleful at a time, stirring between each addition. The sauce should coat the back of a spoon.
Stir in the cream and return the chicken to the pan with any resting juices. Taste and adjust seasoning. The sauce will be a little loose — it thickens further in the oven. Remove from the heat.
Pour the filling into the pie dish and leave to cool for 10 minutes. This prevents the pastry steaming from the underside.

Unroll the pastry and drape it over the dish, pressing the edges firmly against the rim. Trim off any overhang, then crimp the edges with a fork or your fingers. Cut two small steam vents in the centre.
Brush the surface generously with egg wash. Bake for 25–30 minutes until the pastry is deep golden and the filling is bubbling through the vents.

content.text: The key to a crisp pastry lid is a filling that is not piping hot when the pastry goes on. Pour the filling into the dish, then give it ten minutes while you prepare the lid. That short rest prevents steam from softening the underside before it has a chance to set.
Heat the oven to 200 °C fan. Season

with salt and black pepper. Set a large frying pan over a high heat with a film of oil. Brown the chicken in two batches, 3–4 minutes per batch, turning once. Transfer to a plate — the chicken is not cooked through yet, just coloured.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add {{butter-unsalted}} of butter to the same pan. Once foaming, soften the onion for 5 minutes. Add the garlic and thyme leaves for 1 minute, then add {{chestnut-mushrooms}} of mushrooms. Cook, stirring occasionally, for 4–5 minutes until golden and any liquid has evaporated.
Scatter {{plain-flour}} of plain flour over the vegetables and stir for 1 minute to cook out the raw taste. Pour in {{white-wine-dry}} of white wine and stir to lift the
from the base. Let it bubble for a minute, then add {{chicken-stock}} of hot stock gradually, stirring as you go. The sauce should coat the back of a spoon.
Stir in {{double-cream}} of cream. Return the chicken with any resting juices. Taste and adjust seasoning. Remove from the heat and pour into the pie dish. Leave for 10 minutes.

Unroll the cold puff pastry and drape it over the dish, pressing the edges firmly against the rim. Trim any overhang and crimp the edges. Cut two small steam vents in the centre. Brush the surface with
and bake for 25–30 minutes until the pastry is deep golden and the filling bubbles through the vents. Rest for 5 minutes before serving.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Two things that commonly go wrong — and how to prevent them.

attrs.solution: The filling was too hot when the pastry went on. Give the filling at least 10 minutes in the dish before topping. Also check the oven is fully up to temperature before the pie goes in — a cold oven steams rather than bakes the pastry.

attrs.problem: Sauce is too thin after baking
attrs.solution: The sauce needs to be thick enough off the hob to coat the back of a spoon. If it ran loose, return the dish to the oven uncovered for a further 5 minutes, or stir a teaspoon of cornflour slaked in cold water into the filling before topping next time.

content.text: Replace the mushrooms with two thinly sliced leeks. Cook them in butter over a medium-low heat for 8–10 minutes until completely tender before adding the flour. The result is sweeter and milder — good for those who prefer it.
